He closed it behind me and turned the key and looked at me with those deep-set eyes, and in them I saw not sympathy but complicity, the look of one initiate to another, and he said nothing, and I said nothing, and we stood in the churchyard under the stars while the wind moved through the elms, and beneath our feet, beneath the graves and the roots and the bedrock, the sound continued, patient and eternal and slow, the sound of something that had been waiting for a very long time and was content to wait a little longer.

chapters.title: The Moors at Dusk (after Emily Bronte)
chapters.text:
The wind came down from the heights with a sound like mourning, raking through the heather and the dead bracken with fingers that were neither gentle nor cruel but merely indifferent, the fingers of a force that has swept across these moors since before the first stone was laid upon another to make a wall, and will sweep across them long after the last wall has fallen and the last inhabitant has gone to ground beneath the sod that covers everything here, the living and the dead alike, in a blanket of green that is beautiful and relentless and final.

Margaret stood at the edge of the garden, if garden it could be called, for it was little more than a square of earth enclosed by a low wall of dry stone that served less to keep the wilderness out than to mark the boundary between the domestic and the wild, a boundary that was, in this place, more theoretical than practical, the moor encroaching upon the garden and the garden yielding to the moor with a resignation that suggested a long history of defeat. The few plants that survived here were of the tenacious variety, gorse and mountain ash and a rose bush that her mother had planted twenty years ago and that now bore no flowers but still put out leaves each spring with a stubbornness that Margaret admired and that reminded her of her mother, who had been stubborn in the same way, tenacious in the same way, determined to bloom in a place where blooming was an act of defiance against every element.

Her mother had been dead four months. The house behind Margaret, Thornfield, stood dark and silent against the sky, its windows unlit, its chimneys cold. It was a house built for wind, its walls two feet thick, its windows narrow, its roof pitched steeply against the snow that would come in December and remain until March, and it had stood on this spot for two hundred years, absorbing the weather and the grief of the families who had lived in it, until the stone itself seemed saturated with both, so that on certain evenings, when the wind was from the north and the light was failing, the house seemed not merely to contain sorrow but to emanate it, like heat from a fire or light from a lamp, a steady ambient sorrow that was as much a feature of the place as the heather or the rock.

Margaret had come home to close the house. There was no one left to live in it. Her father had died the year before, her brother was in India, her sister had married and moved to the south, to a house near the sea where the air was soft and the wind was a novelty rather than a condition of existence. Margaret herself lived in York, where she taught music at a school for girls, and she had not been home in two years, and now that she was here, now that she stood in the garden that was not a garden and looked out at the moor that was the only landscape of her childhood, she understood that she had stayed away not from negligence or indifference but from fear. Fear of what she would feel. Fear of what she would remember. Fear that the place would claim her, as it had claimed her mother and her father and every Earnshaw before them, drawing them into its rhythm of wind and stone and silence until they became indistinguishable from it, until their bones were part of the bedrock and their voices were part of the wind.

She walked out onto the moor. The path, such as it was, led north from the garden wall toward the ridge that her family called the Edge, a long outcrop of millstone grit that ran for half a mile along the summit of the hill and from which, on clear days, you could see three counties. The heather was dead now, October having stripped it of the purple that covered the moors in late summer like a bruise, and in its place was a landscape of brown and gray, the colors of dormancy, the colors of a world withdrawing into itself in preparation for the long cold months ahead.

The ground was soft underfoot, saturated with rain. Margaret's boots sank with each step, the peat releasing a smell of earth and decomposition that was as familiar to her as the smell of her own skin. She had walked this path a thousand times. As a child, she had run it, fleet and fearless, racing her brother to the Edge, arriving breathless and triumphant at the summit to stand with her arms spread wide and the wind tearing at her hair, feeling herself to be the freest creature in creation, freer than the curlews that wheeled above her, freer than the hares that bolted at her approach, free in the absolute and uncomplicated way that only a child on a hilltop can be free.

Now she walked slowly, conscious of each step, conscious of the weight of the years that separated the child from the woman, the running from the walking, the freedom from the knowledge that freedom is a condition that does not survive the acquisition of understanding. She understood things now that the child had not understood. She understood that her mother's stubbornness was not strength but desperation, the determination of a woman trapped in a place she could not leave and a life she could not change. She understood that her father's silence was not peace but surrender. She understood that the beauty of the moor, which she had loved as a child with a love so fierce it was indistinguishable from need, was a beauty that existed alongside and inseparable from a brutality that the child had been too young to perceive and the woman could no longer ignore.

She reached the Edge as the last light was leaving the sky. The western horizon held a band of color, orange fading to rose fading to violet, the sky's daily valediction, its ceremony of closure, performed with the same unhurried precision each evening regardless of whether anyone was watching. Below her, the moor spread in every direction, undulating and dark, interrupted here and there by the pale line of a stream or the dark mass of a copse of wind-stunted oaks. In the distance, the lights of a farmhouse flickered, a single point of warmth in a landscape that was rapidly being absorbed by the dark.

She sat on the rock that she and her brother had called the Throne, a flat-topped boulder at the highest point of the Edge. The rock was cold through her skirt. The wind pressed against her like a hand. And Margaret, who had not wept at her mother's funeral, who had stood dry-eyed at the graveside while the minister read words about dust and returning and eternal rest, who had accepted condolences with a nod and a pressed hand and an expression that gave nothing away, opened her mouth and released a sound that was swallowed by the wind before it had traveled a yard from her lips.

It was not a cry. It was not a sob. It was something more primitive, more elemental, a sound that came from a place below language, below thought, below the carefully constructed architecture of composure that had carried her through the funeral and the weeks that followed. It was the sound of grief, pure and unmediated, the sound that the body makes when the mind can no longer contain what it is holding, the sound of a vessel overflowing.

She wept. She wept for her mother, who had planted a rose bush in a place where roses could not grow and had tended it for twenty years and had died in the room above the kitchen on a morning in June when the rose bush was, for the first and only time, in bloom. She wept for her father, who had loved the moor with a love so deep it had consumed him, who had walked out one December evening and not come back, and been found the next morning at the Edge, seated on the Throne, frozen, his face turned toward the valley, his expression, they said, one of perfect calm. She wept for her brother, lost to a country he had gone to out of duty and stayed in out of despair. She wept for herself, for the child who had run across these moors with her arms spread wide, who had believed that the wind was her friend and the heather was her carpet and the sky was her roof, and who had been right, and who was gone.

The stars came out. The wind did not abate. The moor, which had absorbed the tears and the silence and the grief of a thousand years of human habitation, absorbed hers without comment, without judgment, without consolation. It offered nothing. It took nothing. It simply was, as it had always been, vast and dark and beautiful and indifferent, and Margaret sat on her rock and let the wind dry her face and understood, for the first time, why her mother had stayed.

It was not stubbornness. It was not desperation. It was this. This moment. This wind. This sky. This precise quality of darkness, which was not the darkness of absence but the darkness of presence, the darkness of a landscape so full of itself that light was superfluous. Her mother had stayed because the moor was the only place in the world that matched the scale of what she felt, the only landscape vast enough to contain the love and the grief and the loneliness and the beauty that were the raw materials of her inner life. She had stayed because she needed the wind, needed the space, needed the knowledge that there existed something larger and more permanent than her own suffering, something that would endure long after she and her roses and her stubbornness had returned to the earth.

Margaret stood. The wind pressed against her. She spread her arms, as she had done as a child, and felt it catch her coat and push against her palms, and for a moment she was the child again, free and fearless on the hilltop, and the woman, weighted with grief and knowledge, and both at once, and the distance between them was not a distance but a continuity, a single line drawn from the running child to the standing woman across a landscape that did not change and did not care and was, in its vast indifference, the closest thing to permanence she would ever know.

She did not close the house. She did not return to York. She wrote to the school and resigned her position. She wrote to her sister and explained, in terms that her sister found alarming and her brother-in-law found incomprehensible, that she was staying. She cleaned the hearths and lit the fires and opened the windows to the wind. She pruned the rose bush, which had survived the summer on neglect and rainwater and the stubborn genetic imperative of its kind. She walked to the Edge each evening and sat on the Throne and watched the light leave the sky, and the darkness come, and the stars appear, and felt the wind, which was the same wind that had dried her tears and scattered her grief across the heather, and which would, she knew, one day scatter her too, her dust and her memory and her love, across the moor that had made her and would unmake her and would remain, long after the last Earnshaw was gone, the same as it had always been: vast and dark and beautiful and indifferent, and hers.

chapters.title: The Summit of Dovre (after Jules Verne)
chapters.text:
Professor Doederlein checked the barometer for the sixth time in an hour and found that the mercury had risen another two millimeters, bringing the atmospheric pressure to a level that, at this altitude of four thousand seven hundred feet above the level of the sea, indicated fair weather with a certainty that no meteorological instrument could guarantee but that was, in the professor's considered opinion, as close to certainty as the sciences of the atmosphere permitted.

"We ascend," he announced to the party assembled in the stone hut that served as the final waystation before the summit of the Dovre range. The party consisted of the professor himself, a man of fifty-three whose appearance suggested a monument that had been exposed to weather, all angles and erosion, his beard trimmed to a point that gave his face the shape of an inverted triangle and his eyes the blue of glacial ice; his niece, Ingrid, a young woman of twenty-six whose presence on the expedition had been the subject of considerable debate among the Norwegian Alpine Club and whose performance on the ascent thus far had silenced every objection; their guide, a man called Knut who spoke little and knew everything and who had been climbing these mountains since the age at which other children were learning to read; and two porters, brothers from Trondheim, who carried the scientific instruments with the careful indifference of men who understood nothing of their purpose but respected their weight.

They set out at half past four in the morning, the dawn still an hour away, the sky above them a dome of stars so brilliant and so numerous that Ingrid, pausing on the threshold of the hut, felt a vertigo that had nothing to do with altitude and everything to do with the sudden apprehension of scale, the awareness that the stars she was looking at were not merely lights but suns, each one attended by its own system of worlds, each system separated from its neighbors by distances that the human mind could represent mathematically but could not genuinely comprehend.

"Come," said Knut. "The snow is firm. We go now."

The route led northward from the hut along a ridge of bare rock that narrowed as it climbed, its edges falling away on either side into darkness. The professor walked behind Knut, his ice axe tapping the rock with each step, his breathing measured and regular, the breathing of a man who has trained his lungs to extract the maximum oxygen from the minimum air. Ingrid followed her uncle, her own breathing less regular but no less determined, her boots finding the footholds that Knut's larger boots had tested and confirmed. The porters brought up the rear, their heavy packs shifting with each step, the brass fittings of the instruments catching the starlight and throwing it back in small bright sparks.

The ridge ascended for perhaps a mile before it reached the snow line. Here the landscape changed abruptly, the dark rock giving way to a surface of compacted snow that gleamed faintly in the starlight, its texture firm and granular, the product of countless cycles of freezing and thawing that had compressed the original snowfall into a substance that was neither ice nor snow but something intermediate, dense enough to support their weight but yielding enough to accept the bite of their crampons.

Knut led them across the snowfield in a line that angled to the northwest, avoiding the steeper direct approach in favor of a traverse that added distance but reduced the gradient to a manageable angle. The professor, who had plotted the route on his maps and calculated the optimal ascent profile using trigonometric methods of his own devising, approved of this choice and said so. Knut said nothing. He had been making this choice for thirty years, long before the professor and his trigonometry had arrived to validate it.

The dawn came at half past five. It began as a thickening of the darkness in the east, a gradual differentiation of the sky from the mountains, as though the world were being drawn by an artist who started with the faintest pencil and pressed harder as he worked. The stars dimmed. The snow, which had been a uniform gray, acquired depth and texture, its surface resolving into a landscape of ridges and hollows and wind-carved formations that threw long shadows in the horizontal light. The mountains to the east emerged from the darkness in stages, first their peaks, then their upper slopes, then their flanks, each one catching the light in turn, a sequence of revelations that proceeded from the highest to the lowest with the inexorable logic of a demonstration.

Ingrid stopped to watch. She had seen sunrises before, from sea level, from the deck of her uncle's research vessel, from the balcony of her apartment in Christiania. But she had never seen a sunrise from four thousand seven hundred feet above the sea, and the difference was not merely one of altitude but of kind. At sea level, the sunrise is a spectacle observed from within the world. At this altitude, it was a spectacle observed from above it, or at least from the edge of it, from a vantage point that was close enough to the ordinary world to recognize it but far enough removed to see it whole, to apprehend it as a totality rather than a collection of details.

The valleys below were still in darkness, filled with a blue-gray mist that made them look like lakes, and from this mist the peaks rose like islands, their summits burning with the new light, and the effect was of a world that was being created at that very moment, assembled from raw materials of light and stone and air, and Ingrid felt, with a conviction that was physical rather than intellectual, that she was witnessing something primordial, something that predated not merely human civilization but human consciousness itself, something that had occurred every morning since the earth first turned its face toward the sun and would continue to occur every morning until the sun itself went dark.

"Magnificent," said the professor, and the word, which was wholly inadequate, was also wholly appropriate, because there was nothing else to say. The English language, which possessed words for every shade of social embarrassment and every nuance of commercial transaction, had produced only a handful of words for this, for the experience of standing on a mountain at dawn and watching the world come into existence, and every one of them fell short, and the falling short was itself a kind of eloquence, an admission that the thing being described was greater than the instrument of description.

They continued upward. The snowfield steepened. Knut cut steps where necessary, his axe biting into the compacted snow with a precision that was the product of decades of practice, each step exactly the right depth and exactly the right distance from the last. The professor followed, placing his feet in the steps with the care of a man who understands that at this altitude, on this angle, a misstep is not merely an inconvenience but a catastrophe, a slide that begins slowly and ends at the bottom of a crevasse or the base of a cliff or in the blue-gray mist of the valley below, which from this height looked soft and welcoming but which was, in fact, two thousand feet of empty air.

At half past seven, they reached the cornice. This was the final obstacle, a lip of overhanging snow that the wind had built along the summit ridge, curling it outward like a wave frozen at the moment of breaking. It was perhaps eight feet high, and Knut studied it for several minutes, probing it with his axe, before selecting a point where the overhang was narrowest and cutting a channel through it, showering the party below with chunks of snow that fell past them and continued falling for what seemed an impossibly long time before vanishing into the mist.

Knut went through first. Then the professor. Then Ingrid. She pulled herself through the channel, the snow cold against her cheek, her gloves gripping the compacted surface, and then she was through, and she stood up, and she was on the summit.

The world opened.

There is no other way to describe what happens when one reaches the summit of a mountain. The landscape, which during the ascent has been a series of fragments, a slope here, a ridge there, the occasional glimpse through a gap in the cloud, suddenly becomes whole. It assembles itself around you in every direction at once, a panorama so complete and so overwhelming that the eye does not know where to settle and the mind does not know how to process what the eye is receiving.

To the north, the mountains continued, peak after peak, stretching toward the Arctic in a procession that diminished with distance until the farthest peaks were indistinguishable from the clouds that attended them. To the south, the land descended toward the fjords, those deep glacial valleys where the mountains met the sea in a conjunction of geological violence that had produced some of the most dramatic topography on the surface of the earth. To the east, the plateau of the Vidda spread like a table, brown and treeless, crossed by rivers that caught the sunlight and flashed silver. To the west, the Atlantic Ocean lay like a sheet of hammered metal, extending to the horizon and beyond it, to Iceland, to Greenland, to the shores of a continent that none of them could see but that they knew was there, waiting on the far side of three thousand miles of water.

The professor produced his instruments. The porters, who had carried them for twelve miles and four thousand vertical feet, set them down with the relief of men putting down a burden they had begun to believe they would carry forever. The barometer was read. The thermometer was consulted. The hygrometer was deployed. The anemometer was erected and immediately began to spin, measuring a wind that the party could feel on their faces but that the professor insisted on quantifying, because quantification was the foundation of science and science was the foundation of understanding and understanding was, in the professor's view, the highest purpose to which a human life could be directed.

Ingrid stood apart from the scientific activity and looked at the world. She looked at it for a long time, turning slowly, taking in the full three hundred and sixty degrees of the panorama. She felt, and would later struggle to articulate, that what she was seeing was not merely a landscape but a statement, a declaration made by the earth itself about the nature of existence, a declaration that was at once simple and profound, reducible to a single proposition: that the world is larger than you. That it was here before you and will be here after you and does not require your presence to justify its existence. That the proper response to it is not analysis or measurement or description but silence, the silence of a creature standing at the edge of something vast, recognizing its own smallness, and finding in that recognition not despair but liberation, not diminishment but the beginning of a perspective from which everything that had previously seemed important becomes exactly as important as it actually is, which is not very.

The professor called to her. There were readings to take, observations to record, data to collect. The summit was a laboratory, and she was his assistant, and there was work to be done. She turned from the panorama and joined him, and the work absorbed her, and the morning passed in measurements and calculations and the careful notation of numbers in a ledger that would be published in the proceedings of the Norwegian Academy of Sciences and read by perhaps two hundred people, none of whom would understand, from the numbers alone, what it had been like to stand on this summit at dawn and watch the world open like a book to a page that had never been read before and would never be read again in exactly the same light.

chapters.title: The Barricade at the Rue de la Chanvrerie (after Hugo)
chapters.text:
The barricade had been standing for sixteen hours when the child appeared.

He came from the direction of the Rue Mondetour, a boy of perhaps twelve, though hunger and the streets had given him the face of an old man compressed into a child's frame. He was barefoot, his feet black with the grime of the cobblestones, and his clothes, such as they were, consisted of a man's shirt that reached his knees and a waistcoat that had once been red and was now the color of dried blood. He carried nothing except a cap, which he wore at an angle that suggested not style but defiance, the angle of a creature who has been beaten by the world and has chosen to respond not with submission but with impudence.

The men at the barricade saw him approach and some of them laughed, not cruelly but with the laughter that comes from recognizing a familiar type, for they had all been this boy, or known him, or lived beside him in the tenements where such boys were produced in quantities that exceeded any conceivable demand. He was a gamin, a child of the streets, one of the thousands of half-feral creatures who inhabited the interstices of Paris like sparrows inhabiting the gaps between buildings, surviving on scraps and cunning and a vitality that the comfortable classes found simultaneously admirable and inconvenient.

"I have come to fight," said the boy.

His voice was high and clear and utterly without hesitation. He addressed the barricade as one might address an institution, a school or a hospital, a place where one has business and expects to be received.

Enjolras, who commanded the barricade and whose beauty and severity gave him the aspect of an angel carved from marble, looked down at the boy from the summit of the fortification, which was composed of overturned carts, paving stones, mattresses, a piano, two doors torn from their hinges, and a quantity of miscellaneous furniture that had been contributed by the residents of the surrounding houses with varying degrees of willingness.

"You are too young," said Enjolras.

"I am old enough to die," said the boy. "If I am old enough for that, I am old enough to fight."

This logic, which was unanswerable, silenced Enjolras for a moment. He looked at the boy, and the boy looked back at him, and between them passed one of those communications that require no words, a recognition that the categories by which the world is normally organized, categories of age and authority and propriety, have been suspended by circumstances that do not respect them. There was a revolution in the streets. The army was assembling at the end of the boulevard. Within hours, perhaps within minutes, the soldiers would advance, and the barricade would be attacked, and some of those who stood behind it would die. In such a moment, the question of whether a twelve-year-old boy was too young to participate became not merely irrelevant but obscene, a nicety imported from a world that had, for the moment, ceased to exist.

"Give him a musket," said Enjolras.

The boy scrambled up the barricade with the agility of a monkey ascending a tree. He was given a musket that was nearly as tall as he was and a cartridge pouch that hung against his thigh like a saddlebag on a pony. He found a position between two paving stones near the summit and peered over the top with an expression of intense concentration, as though the boulevard beyond were a puzzle that he was determined to solve.

The men around him regarded him with a mixture of amusement and tenderness that they would have denied if asked about it and that they expressed in the only way available to men in such circumstances, by practical actions. One of them gave him a piece of bread. Another adjusted the strap of his cartridge pouch. A third showed him how to load the musket, ramming the ball and powder home with a motion that the boy imitated with the quick precision of a natural mimic.

The hours passed. The barricade waited. From beyond the boulevard came the sounds of the army's preparations, the clatter of hooves, the bark of officers' commands, the metallic percussion of weapons being readied. In the houses above, faces appeared at windows, pale and watchful, the faces of citizens who were close enough to touch the revolution but had chosen, for reasons of prudence or fear or a calculation of odds that favored survival over principle, not to join it.

The first attack came at four in the afternoon.

It began with a volley from the soldiers at the end of the boulevard, a simultaneous discharge of fifty muskets that struck the barricade like a fist, sending fragments of stone and wood into the air and filling the street with a cloud of white smoke that smelled of sulfur and hot metal. The sound, which was enormous, echoed off the facades of the surrounding buildings and returned from every direction at once, so that it seemed not like a volley but like an explosion, an event without source or direction, filling the entire auditory field.

The defenders fired back. The barricade erupted with a ragged volley that lacked the precision of the soldiers' fire but compensated with a ferocity that was, in its own way, more intimidating, the ferocity of men who are fighting not for pay or promotion but for an idea, and who know that the penalty for failure is death and have accepted it and have moved beyond acceptance into something that resembled, from a distance, joy.

The boy fired his musket. The recoil knocked him backward, off his feet, and he landed on his back on the interior slope of the barricade with an expression of surprise that lasted exactly one second before being replaced by a grin of such incandescent delight that the men around him, hardened veterans of three revolutions, felt their hearts expand.

He scrambled back to his position, reloaded with hands that shook not from fear but from excitement, and fired again. And again. And again.

The attack was repulsed. The soldiers withdrew, leaving three of their number on the cobblestones, dark shapes in the settling smoke. The defenders cheered. The boy cheered loudest of all, his voice rising above the others like a bird above a flock, high and clear and defiant.

But the victory was temporary, and everyone knew it. The soldiers would regroup. They would bring artillery. The barricade, which could withstand musket fire, could not withstand cannon, and when the cannon came, the piano and the mattresses and the overturned carts would be reduced to splinters, and the men behind them would be reduced to something less than splinters, and the revolution, this revolution, would end as the others had ended, in blood and smoke and the reassertion of the order that revolutions exist to challenge and that has, throughout history, proven remarkably adept at surviving the challenges mounted against it.

Englras knew this. He had known it from the beginning. He had built the barricade not because he believed it would stand but because he believed it should be built, because the act of building it, the act of standing behind it, the act of dying for the idea it represented, was itself the message, was itself the revolution, was itself the future asserting its existence in the teeth of a present that denied it.

The boy did not know this. The boy knew only that he was here, on the barricade, with a musket in his hands and men around him who treated him as an equal, and that for the first time in his life he was part of something larger than himself, something that gave his existence, which had been until this moment a sequence of days to be survived, a meaning and a direction and a dignity that no king or general or policeman could grant and no king or general or policeman could take away.

The second attack began at dusk. This time the soldiers brought a cannon.

The first ball struck the barricade at its base, and the piano, which had survived the musket fire, disintegrated in a shower of keys and strings and splintered wood that sprayed across the interior of the fortification like shrapnel. Two men fell. A third was struck by a piano key that embedded itself in his cheek with a precision that would have been comical in any other context. The boy ducked behind his paving stones and felt the air above him part as something, a fragment of cart or a section of door, passed through the space his head had occupied a moment before.

The cannon fired again. And again. The barricade shook. Pieces of it fell inward. The mattresses, which had provided a measure of protection against musket balls, were useless against artillery, their stuffing erupting through the torn fabric and floating in the air like snow, or like the feathers of a pillow in a children's fight, a comparison that would not have been inappropriate, for there was in this bombardment something of the senseless, gleeful destructiveness of children at play, the same indifference to consequence, the same absorption in the act itself.

Englras gave the order to fall back to the inner barricade, a smaller fortification that had been built as a last redoubt, a position from which to make a final stand when the outer wall fell. The men retreated, dragging their wounded, leaving their dead. The boy went with them, his musket in one hand and his cap in the other, running across the exposed ground between the two barricades with a speed and a recklessness that suggested either extraordinary courage or a complete failure to understand the danger, and it was impossible to tell which, and perhaps there was no difference.

At the inner barricade, they regrouped. There were twenty-three of them now, down from the thirty-eight who had built the outer wall that morning. Ammunition was low. Water was gone. The twilight was deepening, and in the gathering dark the soldiers were advancing, their bayonets fixed, their formation tight, the officers' swords catching the last of the light.

Englras stood on the inner barricade and looked at his men. His face, which had been beautiful in the way that marble is beautiful, was now beautiful in the way that fire is beautiful, illuminated from within by something that was beyond courage, beyond conviction, beyond anything that could be named or classified. He raised his red flag, the flag that had flown above the outer barricade and that was now torn and blackened with powder smoke, and he held it aloft, and the men saw it, and something moved through them, something that was not hope, for hope had left them hours ago, but something better than hope, something older and more durable, a willingness to stand where they were standing and do what they were doing and die if dying was required, not because they expected to win but because they were unable, in the deepest sense, to do otherwise.

The boy stood among them. He was twelve years old. He had been alive for twelve years, and most of those years had been hard, and some of them had been terrible, and none of them had prepared him for this, this moment, this barricade, this flag, this feeling that was coursing through him like a river through a channel too small to contain it. He did not understand the politics. He did not understand the theory. He understood only that he was here, and that these men were here, and that they were standing together against something that was larger and stronger and better armed, and that the standing was the thing, the standing was everything, and whatever happened next, whether they lived or died, whether the revolution succeeded or failed, this moment, this standing, this refusal to move, was an act of such defiant and unreasonable beauty that it justified every hungry night and every cold morning and every blow he had ever received from a world that had never, until this evening, given him anything worth fighting for.

The soldiers advanced. The inner barricade fired its last volley. The smoke rose into the darkening sky. And in the Rue de la Chanvrerie, in the heart of Paris, in the heart of the century, a boy with a musket and a cap worn at a defiant angle stood his ground, and the ground held, and the flag flew, and the night came down.
